narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 p.Ser1140G (c.3418A>G, rs2227945) is a missense change in exon 11 of the BRCA1 gene that has been reported in several cohorts of women with breast lesions or breast cancer, particularly in African and North African populations, but no definitive disease association has been established.

Clinical reports have identified the variant in a small number of breast‑cancer patients but also in control individuals, leading the authors of an African‑American cohort to conclude that it most likely represents a benign polymorphism [10480351]. A Burkinabe study detected the variant among other BRCA1/2 changes in women with breast nodules but did not assign any clinical significance [31203256]. In Libyan breast‑cancer families the variant was listed as benign in the authors’ variant table [PMC11210411]. A Bahraini cohort described the allele as a common benign SNP (approximately 8 % allele frequency) while noting that, in aggregate with other BRCA1/2 SNPs, it may contribute modestly to breast‑cancer risk [PMC6625152]. The Kazakh population study reported a very low minor allele frequency (<0.01) and did not pursue further analysis, providing no pathogenic interpretation [PMC10772761].

Functional investigations have consistently shown behavior compatible with a non‑deleterious allele. In a comprehensive functional landscape of BRCA1 missense variants, S1140G displayed homology‑directed repair activity comparable to wild‑type (≈106 % of WT) and showed no altered sensitivity to cisplatin or olaparib, supporting a benign phenotype [PMC5432210]. The same study noted that the variant is recorded 16–40 times in ClinVar and is classified as benign by both BIC and ClinVar based on genetic and epidemiological evidence. A yeast‑based functional assay included S1140G among tested variants but did not report any functional deficit, and no pathogenic claim was made [PMC6156519].

Population and computational data reinforce the benign interpretation. Reported allele frequencies range from about 6 % in African‑American controls to 8–16 % in Middle‑Eastern cohorts, and as low as <1 % in a Central Asian (Kazakh) sample [10480351, PMC6625152, PMC10772761]. In silico predictions (e.g., SIFT) label the substitution as tolerated, and multiple databases annotate it as benign or likely benign [PMC6625152].

Overall, the preponderance of evidence from clinical screening, functional assays, population frequency studies, and computational predictions indicates that BRCA1 p.Ser1140G is a benign polymorphism with no clear pathogenic role in hereditary breast cancer. While some studies suggest it may modestly influence risk when considered with other low‑penetrance variants, the current data do not support classification as pathogenic.

mentions.quoted_text: Breast cancer is the top cause of cancer mortality among women in the world and the second in Africa. The aims of this study were to: i) identify women with breast nodules suspected of having breast cancer ii) sequence the BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes and iii) screen mutations. From 2015 to 2016, 112 women aged from 35 to 44 years, who had come for consultation in the gynecology/obstetrics and the oncology department of the University Hospital Yalgado Ouedraogo, voluntarily agreed to participate to this study. Whole blood was collected from those with mammary nodules. The genomic DNA was extracted using Qiagen kit. FAST KAPA was used for genomic DNA amplification and the purified PCR products were analyzed by direct sequencing using Big Dye v1.1 and ABI 3730 automated sequencer. Nucleotides substitutions were determined. We identified BRCA1 SNPs rs1799966, rs799917, rs16942, rs16941, **rs2227945**, and BRCA2 SNPs rs169547, rs4986860. These identified variants are found mostly in cases of benign tumors of breast or ovarian cancer with familial history of breast cancer. This study in Burkina-Faso, is the basis for improved and more specific genetic testing, and suggests that additional genes contributing to an increased risk of breast cancer should be analyzed.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identified the BRCA1 p.Ser1140Gly variant among other BRCA1/2 variants in a cohort of Burkinabe women with breast nodules, but did not provide any interpretation of its clinical significance.
article_mentions.title: Identification of BRCA1/2 p.Ser1613Gly, p.Pro871Leu, p.Lys1183Arg, p.Glu1038Gly, p.Ser1140Gly, p.Ala2466Val, p.His2440Arg variants in women under 45 years old with breast nodules suspected of having breast cancer in Burkina Faso.

mentions.quoted_text: The breast cancer predisposing gene, BRCA1, was analyzed for germline mutations in 45 African American families at high-risk for hereditary breast cancer. Patients were considered high-risk if they had a family history of the disease, early onset breast cancer, bilateral breast cancer, or breast and ovarian cancer. The entire BRCA1 coding and flanking intron regions have been examined by single stranded conformation polymorphism analysis followed by sequencing of variant bands. Eleven different BRCA1 germline mutations/variations were identified in 7 patients from the 45 high-risk families. Two pathogenic, protein-truncating mutations were detected in exon 11. A ten base pair tandem duplication, 943ins10, was present in a woman with breast and ovarian cancer whose first-degree relatives had prostate cancer. A four base pair deletion, 3450del4, was detected in a breast cancer patient with five cases of breast cancer in the family; two of the proband's sisters with breast cancer also carried the same mutation. Four amino acid substitutions (Lys1183Arg, Leu1564Pro, Gln1785His, and Glu1794Asp) and four nucleotide substitutions in intron 22 (IVS22+78 C/A, IVS22+67 T/C, IVS22+8 T/A and IVS22+7 T/C) were observed in patients and not in control subjects. One early onset breast cancer patient carried five distinct BRCA1 variations, two amino acid substitutions and three substitutions in intron 22. An amino acid substitution in exon 11, **Ser1140Gly**, was identified in 3 different unrelated patients and in 6 of 92 control samples. The latter probably represents a benign polymorphism.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The BRCA1 S1140G variant was detected in a few patients but also in multiple control individuals, leading the authors to conclude it likely represents a benign polymorphism.
article_mentions.title: BRCA1 mutations in African Americans.

Among the identified SNPs, 5 BRCA1 SNPs (rs1799950 (4%), rs16942 (64%), rs4986850 (20%), **rs2227945** (16%), and rs1799966 (64%)) and 5 BRCA2 SNPs (rs766173 (12%), rs144848 (60%), rs4987117 (4%), rs4987047 (8%), and rs11571833 (4%)) are found to confer minor but cumulatively significant risk of breast cancer (Johnson et al., 2007). In cancer predisposition genes, there are 25 globally known SNPs identified to increase risk of breast cancer and of the 25, 14 SNPs are located in BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes (Johnson et al., 2007). Ten BRCA SNPs out of the 14 BRCA SNPs have been identified in our study subjects.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports the BRCA1 p.Ser1140G variant as a common benign SNP (8% frequency) but also cites literature suggesting it contributes modestly to breast‑cancer risk when considered with other SNPs, reflecting conflicting interpretations of its clinical significance.
article_mentions.title: Characterization of BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic variants in a cohort of Bahraini breast cancer patients using next-generation sequencing

To establish an initial functional landscape of BRCA1 mutations for HR-mediated DSB repair, we constructed 32-patient-derived missense variants along the entire length of the protein (Table 1). The list included 11 variants with known or partially known functional consequences: two in the RING domain (C61G and C64R) that disrupt BARD1 binding, three in the CC motif (M1400V, L1407P and M1411T) that affect PALB2 binding and six in the BRCT domain (S1655F, C1697R, R1699Q, A1708E, S1715R and M1775R) that abrogate BRCA1 interaction with Abraxas, BRIP1 and CtIP (Figure 1B and Figure 1:figure supplement 1). Also included were the top 20-patient-derived missense variants in the BIC database (21 in total due to a tie at the 20th place) and the top 22 in the ClinVar database (Table 1). Additionally, we included four other VUSs, L668F, S1101N, **S1140G** and T1561I, which have now been recorded between 16 and 40 times in ClinVar. As a control for the BRCT missense variants, we also generated a patient-derived frameshift mutation (5055delG or p.Val1646Serfs) that truncates the protein immediately before the BRCT domain.

Notably, our sequencing analysis revealed P871L, E1038G, K1183R and S1613G in the 'wild-type' BRCA1 cDNAs from multiple independent sources. Indeed, they are considered as neutral/benign by both BIC and ClinVar. Among the rest of the top 22 variants in ClinVar, C61G and A1708E are considered pathogenic, and the rest are all considered benign largely based on genetic or epidemiological evidence. Similarly, L668F, S1101N, **S1140G** and T1561I are now also considered as benign. Note that this study was initiated in 2010 largely based on BIC, in which most of the above are now still listed as VUS.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study combines clinical database classifications and functional assays to conclude that the BRCA1 S1140G variant behaves like a benign allele.
article_mentions.title: Functional and mutational landscapes of BRCA1 for homology-directed repair and therapy resistance

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review reports the presence of the BRCA1 S1140G (c.3418A>G) variant among many others in African populations but does not provide interpretation or functional data for it.
article_mentions.title: Genetics of breast cancer in African populations: a literature review

mentions.quoted_text: A total of 14 of the 25 polymorphisms associated with the development of breast cancer are localised in the BRCA genes: 5 - in BRCA1 - rs1799950 (Q356R), rs4986850 (D693N), **rs2227945** (**S1140G**), rs16942 (K1183R), rs1799966 (S1613G); 9 SNP in the gene BRCA2 - rs766173 (N289H), rs144848 (N372H), rs4987117 (T1915M), rs1799954 (R2034C), rs11571746 (S2835P), rs11571747 (E2856A), rs4987047 (I2944F), rs11571833 (K3326), rs1801426 (I3412V).

Analysis of minor allele frequencies of 14 polymorphisms in BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes in the Kazakh population according to the genomic database of the "Miras" DNA Biobank found low (less than 0.01) MAF for **rs2227945** polymorphisms in the BRCA1 gene, and for 5 polymorphisms in the BRCA2 gene - rs1799954, rs11571746, rs11571747, rs4987047 and rs11571833. This prevented these six SNPs from being included in further analysis. Additionally included are rs1800709 (R841W), rs4986852 (S1040N), rs1799949 (S694S) and rs799917 (P871L) polymorphisms in the BRCA1 gene, which have been shown to have significant associations with breast cancer risk in different ethnic populations according to GWAS. Table 1 shows the genetic characteristics of 12 polymorphisms of BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes, indicating the SNP Identifier, the location of the polymorphism on the chromosome, the physical distance at the paired ends (base-pair position - bp) and gene name.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study notes that the BRCA1 S1140G (rs2227945) variant has a low frequency in the Kazakh population and was not included in deeper analyses, offering no functional or clinical interpretation of its significance.
article_mentions.title: Frequencies of Diagnostically Significant Polymorphisms of Hereditary Breast Cancer Forms in BRCA1 and BRCA2 Genes in the Kazakh Population
